items.question: What is the primary purpose of an experiment (run) ID?
options: To replace hyperparameter names in code
To uniquely identify one training run and retrieve its logged data
To encrypt model artifacts at rest
To set the learning rate automatically
items.answer: To uniquely identify one training run and retrieve its logged data
items.explanation: The run ID is the unique key for params, metrics, tags, and artifacts for that execution.

items.question: Which statement about tags is correct?
options: Each tag can appear on only one run globally
Tags are system-generated and cannot be edited
Tags are user-defined labels; many runs can share the same tag
Tags replace the need for a model registry
items.answer: Tags are user-defined labels; many runs can share the same tag
items.explanation: Tags group runs for search; unlike IDs, they are not required to be unique.

items.question: When comparing two runs, what makes comparison misleading?
options: Both runs have unique experiment IDs
Both runs logged metrics under different key names for the same quantity
Both runs used the same evaluation split
Both runs recorded git commit as a parameter
items.answer: Both runs logged metrics under different key names for the same quantity
items.explanation: Inconsistent metric keys prevent fair side-by-side comparison in tracking UIs.

items.question: What does a registered model version in a registry typically link to?
options: A source run ID and artifact path from experiment tracking
Every run in the project automatically
The random seed used in production inference only
items.answer: A source run ID and artifact path from experiment tracking
items.explanation: Registry versions preserve lineage: named version → artifact → training run that produced it.
